coredata.dc:title: Al-based metal-organic gels for selective fluorescence recognition of hydroxyl nitro aromatic compounds
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Mao Xia
Yang, Liu
Jiang, Zhong Wei
Peng, Zhe Wei
Li, Yuan Fang
coredata.dc:description: The novel class of luminescent Al3+-based metal-organic gels (Al-MOGs) have been developed by mix 4-[2,2′:6′,2″-terpyridine]-4′-ylbenzoic acid (Hcptpy) with Al3+ under mild condition. The as-prepared Al-MOGs have not only multiple stimuli-responsive properties, but selective recognition of hydroxyl nitro aromatic compounds, which can quench the fluorescence of the Al-MOGs, while other nitro aromatic analogues without hydroxyl substitutes cannot. The fluorescence of Al-MOGs at 467nm was seriously quenched by picric acid (PA) whose lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy levels are lower than those of three other hydroxyl nitro aromatic compounds including 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), 3,5-dinitrosalicylic acid (3,5-DNTSA) and 2,4-dinitrophenol (2,4-DNP). Thus, PA was chosen as a model compound under optimal conditions and the relative fluorescence intensity of Al-MOGs was proportional to the concentration of PA in the range of 5.0–320.0μM with a detection limit of 4.64μM. Furthermore, the fluorescence quenching mechanism has also been investigated and revealed that the quenching was attributed to inner filter effects (IFEs), as well as electron transfer (ET) between Al-MOGs and PA.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal-organic gels
Fluorescence
Nitro aromatic compounds
Stimuli-responsive

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selective Recognition of Hydroxyl Nitro Aromatic Compounds

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photographs of Al-MOGs under day light (a) and 365nm UV light (b). SEM (c) and TEM (d) images of Al-MOGs. SEM image of the xerogels (e).
Chemical stimuli-responsive fluorescence changes and phase transitions of Al-MOGs. The photographs on the left were taken under day light and the right were taken under 365nm UV light.

(a) The fluorescence emission intensity of as-prepared Al-MOGs with different nitro aromatic compounds (300μM) at RT (λex =380nm; λem =467nm). (b) The fluorescence emission spectra of the Al-MOGs and Al-MOGs with the hydroxyl nitro aromatic compounds (300μM).

(a) The fluorescence emission spectra of the Al-MOGs in the different PA concentrations. Insets are the photographs of Al-MOGs in the different PA concentrations under daylight (left) and 365nm UV light (right). (b) Calibration curve corresponding to the fluorescence intensity for varying concentrations of PA. Insert shows a linear calibration curve from (R2 =0.9954).

(a) Fluorescence decay curves of Al-MOGs in the absence and presence of PA (20μM). (b) The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of all nitro aromatic compounds (100μM) and the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of Al-MOGs. (c) HOMO and LUMO energies (in hartree unit) of the hydroxyl nitro aromatic compounds. (d) Cyclic voltammograms of the Al-MOGs and Al-MOGs with 200μM PA.
Al-MOGs based paper sensor for PA recognition: From left to right: control; 1.0×10−5 M; 5.0×10−5 M; 1.0×10−4 M; 2.0×10−4 M. The photographs were taken under a UV lamp (λex =365nm).

head.ce:title: Al-based metal-organic gels for selective fluorescence recognition of hydroxyl nitro aromatic compounds

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The novel class of luminescent Al3+-based metal-organic gels (Al-MOGs) have been developed by mix 4-[2,2′:6′,2″-terpyridine]-4′-ylbenzoic acid (Hcptpy) with Al3+ under mild condition. The as-prepared Al-MOGs have not only multiple stimuli-responsive properties, but selective recognition of hydroxyl nitro aromatic compounds, which can quench the fluorescence of the Al-MOGs, while other nitro aromatic analogues without hydroxyl substitutes cannot. The fluorescence of Al-MOGs at 467nm was seriously quenched by picric acid (PA) whose lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy levels are lower than those of three other hydroxyl nitro aromatic compounds including 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), 3,5-dinitrosalicylic acid (3,5-DNTSA) and 2,4-dinitrophenol (2,4-DNP). Thus, PA was chosen as a model compound under optimal conditions and the relative fluorescence intensity of Al-MOGs was proportional to the concentration of PA in the range of 5.0–320.0μM with a detection limit of 4.64μM. Furthermore, the fluorescence quenching mechanism has also been investigated and revealed that the quenching was attributed to inner filter effects (IFEs), as well as electron transfer (ET) between Al-MOGs and PA.

The novel class of luminescent Al3+-based metal-organic gels (Al-MOGs) have been developed using 4-[2,2′:6′,2″-terpyridine]-4′-ylbenzoic acid (Hcptpy) and Al3+ through various noncovalent interactions. The as-prepared Al-MOGs have multiple stimuli-responsive properties and selective recognition of hydroxyl nitro aromatic compounds, which can quench the fluorescence of the Al-MOGs, while other nitro aromatic analogues without hydroxyl substitutes cannot. The fluorescence quenching of Al-MOGs is attributed to inner filter effects (IFEs), as well as electron transfer (ET) between Al-MOGs and PA.
Image 2

ce:section.ce:para: Metal-organic gels (MOGs) [1], constructed by metal ions and suitable organic ligands through various noncovalent interactions such as ionic attraction, π-π stacking, hydrogen bonding and van der Waals interaction [2–4], have attracted a lot of attentions. MOGs have been widely applied to chiral recognition [5], proton conduction, catalysis [6], adsorption [7], and fluorescence sensor [8] due to their extraordinarily high surface area, good surface accessibility, permeability, stimuli-responsive properties and rapid mass transfer over the past several decades [9–11]. Among all these applications, fluorescence sensors have attracted a great deal of attentions because of its low cost, high sensitivity and good selectivity [12–14]. In this background, some of MOGs have been used as fluorescence sensors for the detection of cations [15], anions [16], small molecular organics [9], and biomacromolecules [17].

Hydroxyl nitro aromatic compounds such as 4-Nitrophenol (4-NP), 3,5-Dinitrosalicylic acid (3,5-DNTSA), 2,4-Dinitrophenol (2,4-DNP) and picric acid (PA),which used as primary constituent of many known chemical explosives [18,19], are widely used in the war [20,21]. Besides, they have great effects on human health and cause serious illnesses, for instance, irritation of the skin and eyes, even damage of the respiratory organs [22,23]. Therefore, a wide range of methods such as mass spectrometry [24], electrochemical [25], X-ray imaging [26], and surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [27] have been used for detecting hydroxyl nitro aromatic compounds [28–30]. Currently, spectrofluorometry has been effectively applied to detect of hydroxyl nitro aromatic compounds by developing new fluorescent sensors [31–33]. Whereas most of the fluorescent sensors such as metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), nanoparticles, oligomers and chelating organic molecules are not easily accessible due to the complicated synthesis methods [34,35] and poor reproducibility [36]. As an alternative approach, MOGs exhibit more advantages in terms of mild synthesis, convenience, and stability. However, there were few reports about MOGs used for detection of hydroxyl nitro aromatic compounds [8,37]. Barman and coauthors have reported a luminescent Al-based metal-organic gels, which were used to detect PA, but the poor selectivity was an issue to be taken into account since other analogues would interfere with the determination. Further developing stable, convenient and highly selective methods used for detection of hydroxyl nitro aromatic compounds is a worthwhile yet challenging undertaking [8,37].

Herein, we have developed the novel luminescent metal-organic gels (Al-MOGs) by simply mix 4-[2,2′:6′,2″-terpyridine]-4′-ylbenzoic acid (Hcptpy) with Al3+, which show multiple physical and chemical stimuli-responsive properties. On account of the inner filter effect (IFE), the Al-MOGs present good selectivity and sensitivity for detection of hydroxyl nitro aromatic compounds among a set of 15 analogues. And PA is the most sensitive compound, which has lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy level than other three hydroxyl nitro aromatic compounds.

All reagents were analytical grade, commercial and used without further purification. 4-[2,2′:6′,2″-terpyridine]-4′-ylbenzoic acid (Hcptpy, 97%) were obtained from Shanghai UCHEN Inc. Nitro aromatic compounds solutions were prepared using ethanol and other solutions were prepared using ultra-pure water (18.2MΩ).

The morphology of Al-MOGs was characterized by an S-4800 scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Hitachi, Japan). Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) characterization was performed on JEOL JEM-1200EX TEM instrument (JEOL, Japan). The fluorescence and absorption spectra were recorded with a Hitachi F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) and a Hitachi UV-3010 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan), respectively. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area was determined by N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms obtained at 77K on ASAP 2020, USA. Powder X-ray diffraction (XPRD) patterns was collected on an X-Pert3 Powder with a Cu sealed tube (λ=1.5406Å). Thermo gravimetric analysis (TGA) was measured with a METTLER TGA/DSC 1 SF/1382 (NETZSCH, Germany). Elemental analysis was performed on a Carlo-Erba-1106 instrument. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a FITI-8400 (Shimadzu, Japan) in the range of 4000–500cm−1 using the KBr disk method at room temperature (RT). The xerogels were obtained by a Cool safe 110-4 freeze-drying apparatus (Labogene, Denmark). Cyclic voltammograms were performed with a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation (CH Instruments, Shanghai, China). The fluorescence lifetime of Al-MOGs were measured with a FL-TCSPC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, France).

Firstly, 5mg of Hcptpy was dispersed in 0.5mL H2O with 6μL triethylamine (TEA), and then 0.5mL Al3+ solution (5mg Al2(SO4)3·18 H2O dissolved in 0.5mL H2O) was rapidly injected into ligand solution. The mixtures were subsequently standing for 5min at RT after intensive mixing. Finally, the Al-MOGs were evaluated based on the tube inversion test (Fig. 1a).

A certain amount of nitro aromatic compounds solution were added into 1mL Al-MOGs. Then, the mixture directly used for fluorescence detection after mix them thoroughly (λex =380nm; λem =467nm; voltage=400V).

A certain amount of Al-MOGs was evenly spread on the circular filter papers (Diameter is about 0.6cm) and air-dried at RT. Then, different concentrations of PA were dropped on filter papers directly. Finally, the filter papers with PA and realize the convenient visual recognition of PA under a UV lamp (λex =365nm).

We investigated the synthesis conditions of Al-MOGs including the molar ratio of metal: ligand, concentration of TEA, solvent and cation source. The molar ratio of metal:ligand=1:1 was the best synthesis ratio (Fig. S1) and TEA, which could act as cosolvent to dissolve the ligand, was confirmed that the optimum concentration was 43mM (Fig. S2). Precipitation or solution could be observed in the pure organic solvents such as CH3COCH3, THF, CH3CH2OH and DMF. However, Al-MOGs formed in the mixed solvents of high water-content or pure water (Table S1). Obviously, water was the desirable solvent to favour the gelation process and it can be explained with Kamlet-Taft solvent parameter [38]. The parameter used for describe the superiority of the water molecules in extensive formation of hydrogen bonding through its higher α (hydrogen bond donation ability) value (α=1.17). Then, the other two aluminum salts of AlCl3·6H2O and Al(NO3)3·9H2O were used to gelate with Hcptpy in water under the same conditions mentioned above. It was found that the Al-MOGs were formed successfully (Table S1).

Al-MOGs are lilac (Fig. 1a) under day light, while show strong blue fluorescence under the irradiation of 365nm UV light (Fig. 1b). And the fluorescence intensity of Al-MOGs was dozens of times higher than ligand (Fig. S3). SEM (Fig. 1c) and TEM (Fig. 1d) images showed that the Al-MOGs are constructed by nanofiber via molecules self-assembled of gelator. Xerogels, which could be obtained by a vacuum freeze-drying method, were composed of nanoparticles and interconnected to form a porous gel matrix (Fig. 1e). The BET specific surface area (3.6m2/g) and nitrogen sorption curve of the xerogels were also investigated (Fig. S4). The presence of C, O, S, N and Al in the xerogels was confirmed by energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) analysis (Fig. S5). TGA profile indicated that 90% weight of Al-MOGs decreased between 25°C and 100°C, which resulted from the loss of free water. Also, the bound water lost after 100°C and ligand began to decompose at 280°C, which can be analyzed from the trace of Al-MOGs pretreated at 100°C and 200°C for 6h respectively (Fig. S6). As shown in FTIR spectrum (Fig. S7), the weakening of characteristic vibration of terpyridine group at 1400cm−1 in Hcptpy and the presence of Al-N chemical bond at 613cm−1 after gel formation indicate that the combination between Hcptpy and Al3+ was occurred [39]. A new peak at 1120cm−1 in the xerogels, which was ascribed to the characteristic of SO4 2−, was also demonstrated the presence of Al3+ salt. To gain deeper understanding for the assembly process, Al-MOGs was further confirmed by XRD (Fig. S8). A wide-angel diffraction peak at 2θ=25° corresponded to a crystal line interplanar spacing of 3.6Å (based on Bragg's equation), which was conform to the typical π-π stacking distance and suggested the key role of π-π stacking in the information of Al-MOGs [40].

Due to the complex noncovalent interactions and flexible metal coordination, the smart MOGs possess the potential sensitive stimuli-responsive properties compared to other supramolecular gels [9,10]. The physical stimuli-responsive properties of the Al-MOGs were characterized (Fig. S9). Firstly, the Al-MOGs were exposed to an ultrasonic bath (59kHz) at RT for 5h and the result showed that Al-MOGs exhibit favourable stability. Thermal stability experiment indicated that the structure and colour of Al-MOGs showed no visible change when the temperature is less than 60°C, while there would be colour and fluorescence alter of Al-MOGs when the temperature is over 60°C. This phenomenon may be caused by the hydrolysis of metal ion at high temperature. For the shaking stimulation, Al-MOGs were easily transformed into a free-flowing viscous liquid by shaking the EP tube for a few seconds and it would be reverted back to the gel-state after resting for 4–5h.

In addition, the chemical stimuli-responsive properties of Al-MOGs were also investigated in Fig. 2 . Al-MOGs instantly transformed to a colourless transparent solution while the fluorescence intensity also disappeared after injecting 50μL 2mol/L NaOH. The reversible reaction appeared after adding the same amount of HCl. This reversible chemical stimuli-responsive property attribute to the formation and disappearance of AlO2 −. If the HCl was injected into the Al-MOGs firstly, the structure and colour of Al-MOGs would be changed and the fluorescence also be quenched. However, the Al-MOGs show an irreversible reaction after addition the same amount of NaOH. This phenomenon may be caused by the generation of pyridine hydrochloride class substances between Hcptpy and HCl [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective Recognition of Hydroxyl Nitro Aromatic Compounds
ce:section.ce:para: It was found that the fluorescence of Al-MOGs would be quenched by four hydroxyl nitro aromatic compounds including 4-NP, 3,5-DNTSA, 2,4-DNP and PA, while the fluorescence of Al-MOGs cannot be quenched by non-hydroxyl nitro aromatic compounds such as 2-Nitrotoluene (2-NT), Nitrobenzene (NB), 4-Nitrotoluene (4-NT), 3-Nitrobenzoic acid (3-NBA), 1, 3-Dinitrobenzene (1,3-DNB), 2, 4-Dinitrotoluene (2,4-DNT), 3-Nitrophthalic acid (3-NPA), 4-Nitrophenyl acetic acid (4-NPAA), 4-Nitrobenzoic acid (4-NBA), p-Nitrobenzaldehyde (p-NBA) and 2,6-Dinitrotoluene (2,6-DNT) (Fig. 3a). Interestingly, the quenching effect of four hydroxyl nitro aromatic compounds on Al-MOGs is different under the same concentration and PA display the greatest quenching effect (Fig. 3b). Thus, PA is investigated here in detail as a representative based on the fluorescence quenching of Al-MOGs.

To obtain the optimum detection conditions, temperature and proper alkaline environment were discussed. Results proved that the alkaline condition and temperature within a certain range have no effect on PA detection (Figs. S10, S11). Fig. 4a shows the fluorescence intensity of Al-MOGs gradually decreased with the increase of PA concentration, and the maximum emission wavelength had a red-shift from 467 to 477nm, which was explained by the protonation effect of pyridine nitrogen [42]. Under the optimal assay conditions, the standard curve for PA was constructed within the wide range of 5.0–320.0μM with a detection limit of 4.64μM (Fig. 4b). It is worth to note that the morphology of Al-MOGs were not destroyed with the injection of PA in our detection system even with high concentration (Fig. S12).

The possible quenching mechanisms of the fluorescent materials are as follows: dynamic quenching effect (DQE), static quenching effect (SQE) [43], fluorescence resonant energy transfer (FRET) [44], electron transfer (ET) [45] and IFE [46]. To explore the quenching mechanism between PA and Al-MOGs, corresponding experiments were conducted to evaluate each of these quenching effects.

Generally, DQE and SQE are suitable for the small organic molecule quenching process [47]. However, the fluorescence quenching mechanism in terms of nanoscale MOGs in our work is more likely to be ET or IFE, just as the previous reports about metal-organic frameworks [48] and nanofiber [49], which attribute to the same scale as MOGs. Thus, fluorescence decay curves for Al-MOGs in the absence and presence of PA (Fig. 5a) were conducted to investigate the fluorescence quenching mechanism. It can be seen that the lifetime of the Al-MOGs are almost no changes before (29.4ns) and after (29.1ns) adding PA. This result confirmed that there was no FRET process, which is accompanied by changes in the lifetime of fluorescence donor, and coordination occurring between Al-MOGs and PA [50–51]. In addition, the absorption spectrum (Fig. S13) of Al-MOGs, PA as well as the mixture has been studied and we could not observe any new peaks or shift of the maximum absorption with respect to the free Al-MOGs. Meanwhile, the respective UV absorption spectra of all the nitro aromatic compounds also have been studied (Fig. 5b). Just as shown, the extensive absorption band of PA has overlapped with excitation (380nm) of Al-MOGs. No changes in lifetime and absorption band overlapped are indicated that there was IFE causing fluorescence quenching of Al-MOGs [51]. It means that a competition of absorption of the light source energy happen between PA and Al-MOGs largely. The PA could filter the light absorbed by Al-MOGs, resulting in decreased energy absorbed by Al-MOGs, thereby the fluorescence intensity of Al-MOGs was decreased. And the similar quenching process has been reported before [48]. The absorption spectrums of other hydroxyl nitro aromatic compounds such as 4-NP, 3,5-DNTSA and 2,4-DNP were investigated and we found that the quenching degree increased while the overlap degree of absorption peak increased. It is comprehensible that PA, which has an absorption overlap degree superior to other hydroxyl nitro aromatic compounds at the excitation wavelength of Al-MOGs, resulted in a better IFE effect. However, non-hydroxyl nitro aromatic compounds showed no spectral overlap with Al-MOGs and neither could be involved in the competition of absorption of the light source energy with Al-MOGs. Thus, non-hydroxyl nitro aromatic compounds cannot quench the fluorescence of Al-MOGs.

The fluorescence quenching also explained by the LUMO energy level of the hydroxyl nitro aromatic compounds which calculated by density functional theory (DFT) at the B3LYP/6-31G level [52]. From the calculations, we got the energy of the HOMO and LUMO of the hydroxyl nitro aromatic compounds (Fig. 5c). It is clear that the lowest LUMO energy is observed from PA. From this theoretical interpretation, it is also understandable that the fluorescence quenching efficiency is poor for 4-NP, 3,5-DNTSA and 2,4-DNP. Besides, cyclic voltammograms of the Al-MOGs in the absence and presence of PA (Fig. 5d) were also showed that ET between Al-MOGs and PA is occurred. To sum up, the mechanism of both IFE and ET presumably dominates the whole suppression process of Al-MOGs caused by PA.

The test papers were developed in order to realize the convenient visual recognition of PA. Thus, a certain amount of Al-MOGs was evenly spread on the filter papers and then air-dried at RT. The test papers displayed intense blue fluorescence under 365nm UV light without PA. As shown in Fig. 6 , the fluorescence intensity of the test papers gradually decreased with the increase of dropped PA concentration.

In summary, the strong blue fluorescence metal-organic gels, which responsive to multiple physical and chemical stimuli-responsive including ultrasonic, heat, shaking, acid and alkali, were developed. The as prepared Al-MOGs were successfully used for recognition of hydroxyl nitro aromatic compounds with high sensitivity and selectivity. Moreover, fluorescence test papers was prepared and realized the visual recognition of PA. On the strength of this strategy, the Al-MOGs can be used as an alternative platform for hydroxyl nitro aromatic compounds detection in the social and environmental security.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, No. 21575117).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2017.05.057.
